tree_topic: Shopping > Entertainment Media > DVD & Video Shopping
questions: Two major U.S. Supreme Court decisions—one in the 1980s involving home videotape recording technology, and another in the early 2010s involving the resale of foreign-made textbooks—significantly shaped the legal doctrine that allowed video rental shops to operate profitably. Identify both cases and explain: (1) the core legal question in each dispute, (2) how the Court ruled, and (3) how each decision strengthened or clarified the “first sale” principle that enabled businesses such as video rental stores to rent or resell legally purchased media. Additionally, describe at least one long-term impact each ruling had on the broader media or publishing industries.

The Supreme Court’s recognition of the “first sale” doctrine helped make video rental shops possible by allowing businesses to rent out lawfully purchased tapes and DVDs. Decades later, as media shifted from physical discs to downloads, several companies and a start-up attempted to apply similar resale principles to digital goods. Identify the two major technology retailers that each sought patents for systems enabling consumers to resell or transfer digital media they had purchased, as well as the start-up that was sued by a major record label for facilitating the resale of digital music files. For each of the three, describe (1) how their proposed or implemented system ensured that only one copy of a digital file existed at a time, and (2) the specific legal or industry reaction they faced.

logic_chain: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax
rounds.question: Two major U.S. Supreme Court decisions—one in the 1980s involving home videotape recording technology, and another in the early 2010s involving the resale of foreign-made textbooks—significantly shaped the legal doctrine that allowed video rental shops to operate profitably. Identify both cases and explain: (1) the core legal question in each dispute, (2) how the Court ruled, and (3) how each decision strengthened or clarified the “first sale” principle that enabled businesses such as video rental stores to rent or resell legally purchased media. Additionally, describe at least one long-term impact each ruling had on the broader media or publishing industries.
checklist_width: Description of the 1984 home videotape recording case’s core legal issue (whether VCR manufacturers were liable for copyright infringement)
Explanation of the 5–4 ruling allowing the technology and recognition of time-shifting as lawful
Connection between the 1984 decision and the growth of the video rental market (e.g., Blockbuster, DVD/VHS rental industry)
Description of the 2013 textbook resale case’s core legal issue (importation and resale of foreign-manufactured copyrighted works)
Explanation of the 6–3 ruling affirming the right to resell legally purchased goods under first sale doctrine
Discussion of at least one broader impact of the 2013 ruling (effects on publishers, global pricing, digital licensing, or secondary markets)
checklist_depth: Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios (1984) + Identified as the Supreme Court case that upheld VCR legality and enabled video rental growth
Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. (2013) + Identified as the Supreme Court case affirming first sale rights for imported copyrighted works
rounds.rationale: The question uses a deep logical filter by describing two Supreme Court rulings through their factual circumstances (home videotaping technology in the 1980s and foreign textbook resale in the 2010s) without naming them, requiring the agent to correctly identify the cases. It then applies wide aggregation by demanding multiple layers of detail for each case: the legal issue, vote outcome, doctrine applied, and long-term industry impact. The topic is anchored in the video rental shop domain, as both rulings shaped the legal environment that allowed rental and resale businesses to operate.
source_nodes: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax - The New York Times
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Business | Copyright Ruling Rings With Echo of Betamax https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/27/business/in-a-copyright-ruling-the-lingering-legacy-of-the-betamax.html Advertisement
rounds.answer_a:
Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc. (1984) and Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. (2013) are the two landmark cases.

1. Sony (the “Betamax” case)  
   • Core question: Did selling home video recorders that consumers used to copy broadcast TV constitute contributory copyright infringement? [1]  
   • Ruling: 5-4, the Court said household “time-shifting” was fair use and Sony was not liable because the device was “capable of substantial non-infringing use.” [1]  
   • Effect on first sale/rental: By assuring that VCRs could be lawfully sold, the decision let retailers buy tapes once and then rent them repeatedly under the first-sale doctrine, creating the video-rental industry.  
   • Long-term impact: It legitimized consumer recording technologies, paving the way for DVRs, MP3 players, and later streaming-device innovation without immediate liability fears. [2]

2. Kirtsaeng  
   • Core question: Could a U.S. publisher stop a student from reselling lawfully bought textbooks that were printed abroad on the ground that §109(a) first-sale rights applied only to copies made in the United States? [3]  
   • Ruling: 6-3, the Court held that first-sale rights apply to any legally made copy regardless of manufacturing location; Kirtsaeng’s resale was lawful. [3]  
   • Effect on first sale/rental: The decision removed geographic limits, confirming that stores could import legitimate foreign DVDs, books, or games and rent or resell them just like domestically produced copies.  
   • Long-term impact: Publishers lost the ability to sustain international price discrimination; textbook and entertainment firms shifted toward digital distribution and access codes to regain control over secondary markets. [4]

Together, the cases preserved robust first-sale rights that undergird secondary markets for physical media, enabling video rental shops and later used-media businesses to flourish. (242 words)

## References  
[1] Supreme Court of the United States, Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc., 464 U.S. 417 (1984). https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/464/417/  
[2] Farhad Manjoo, “How the Betamax Case Shaped Technology,” The New York Times, April 18, 2014. https://www.nytimes.com/2014/04/20/technology/how-the-betamax-case-shaped-technology.html  
[3] Supreme Court of the United States, Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013). https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/568/519/  
[4] Julie Bosman, “Supreme Court Rules for Student in Textbook Case,” The New York Times, March 19, 2013. https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/20/business/media/supreme-court-rules-first-sale-applies-to-books-made-abroad.html
rounds.answer_b:
The two cases were **Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.** (1984) and **Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.** (2013) [1][2].

In **Sony**, the core question was whether home users’ recording of free over-the-air television on Betamax machines (“time-shifting”) was copyright infringement, and whether Sony could be secondarily liable for selling the VCR [1]. The Court ruled 5–4 for Sony, holding that private, noncommercial time-shifting was fair use and that a device with substantial noninfringing uses could lawfully be sold [1]. Although not a first-sale case in the narrowest sense, Sony protected the legality of VCR technology itself, which made prerecorded videocassettes a viable consumer product. That, combined with first sale, allowed stores to buy legitimate tapes and rent them out without needing separate permission from copyright owners. Long term, **Sony** helped create the home video market, fueling video rental chains and later influencing doctrine protecting multipurpose technologies [1].

In **Kirtsaeng**, the question was whether the Copyright Act’s first-sale doctrine applied to copies lawfully made abroad, specifically cheaper foreign editions of textbooks resold in the United States [2]. The Court ruled 6–3 that first sale does apply to lawfully made foreign-manufactured copies [2]. This clarified that once a copyright owner sells a lawful copy, its control over downstream distribution is generally exhausted, regardless of where the copy was manufactured [2][3]. That ruling strongly reinforced the legal foundation for rental and resale businesses by preventing copyright owners from using manufacturing location to block secondary markets. Long term, **Kirtsaeng** protected libraries, used-book sellers, and retailers dealing in imported copyrighted goods, while constraining publishers’ ability to segment global markets through copyright-based resale restrictions [2][3].

## References
1. Oyez, “Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc. (1984),” https://www.oyez.org/cases/1983/81-1687  
2. Oyez, “Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. (2013),” https://www.oyez.org/cases/2012/11-697  
3. Legal Information Institute, Cornell Law School, “Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.,” https://www.law.cornell.edu/supct/cert/11-697
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios (1984) and Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons (2013), satisfying the core entity requirement. 

Next, Width/Completeness: Both responses addressed the core legal question, the Court’s ruling (5–4 and 6–3 respectively), the connection to the first-sale doctrine, and at least one long-term industry impact for each case. Neither omitted any major checklist element.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: While both are strong, Agent A provides slightly clearer structural segmentation with numbered sections and bullet points under each case, making it easier to scan specific sub-questions (core issue, ruling, effect on first sale, long-term impact). Agent A also more explicitly ties Sony to the operational legality of rental stores under first sale and provides somewhat richer industry context (e.g., digital distribution shift). Citation density is also marginally higher. 

Because both are factually accurate and comprehensive, but Agent A offers marginally better structure and clarity, A is better on user experience grounds. No checklist failures for Agent B.

Revolution in Resale of Digital Books and Music
rounds.question: The Supreme Court’s recognition of the “first sale” doctrine helped make video rental shops possible by allowing businesses to rent out lawfully purchased tapes and DVDs. Decades later, as media shifted from physical discs to downloads, several companies and a start-up attempted to apply similar resale principles to digital goods. Identify the two major technology retailers that each sought patents for systems enabling consumers to resell or transfer digital media they had purchased, as well as the start-up that was sued by a major record label for facilitating the resale of digital music files. For each of the three, describe (1) how their proposed or implemented system ensured that only one copy of a digital file existed at a time, and (2) the specific legal or industry reaction they faced.
checklist_width: Retailer 1: Description of its patented digital ‘exchange’ or ‘data store’ system
Retailer 1: Explanation that resale would delete the file from the original owner’s account
Retailer 1: Industry concern or reaction from publishers/media companies
Retailer 2: Description of its patent application for transferring (not reproducing) digital files
Retailer 2: Mechanism ensuring only one user possesses the file at a time
Retailer 2: Noted industry or public reaction to its proposal
Start-up: Description of its platform for reselling digital music files
Start-up: Explanation of its one-copy-at-a-time technological safeguard
Start-up: Identification of the lawsuit by a major record label and the court’s response (e.g., denial of preliminary injunction)
checklist_depth: Amazon + Sought and received a patent for a digital resale marketplace resembling a swap meet, tied to first-sale style logic
Apple + Filed a patent application for a digital marketplace allowing transfer of e-books, music, movies, and software without duplication
ReDigi + Start-up that enabled resale of iTunes songs and was sued by Capitol Records for copyright infringement
rounds.rationale: The question uses the historical logic of the first-sale doctrine that enabled video rental shops (Deep reasoning filter) to frame a modern parallel in digital media resale. It does not name the entities, requiring identification through legal and technological context. It then requires aggregation of multiple scattered details—technical mechanisms and legal reactions—across three separate entities (Wide scope), ensuring multi-document synthesis.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Technology | Imagining a Swap Meet for E-Books and Music https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/08/technology/revolution-in-the-resale-of-digital-books-and-music.html Advertisement C
rounds.answer_a:
Amazon, Apple and the start-up ReDigi each tried to graft first-sale principles onto the download era.

Amazon  
1) In 2013 it received U.S. Patent 8,364,595 for a “secondary market for digital objects.”  The scheme stored every e-book, MP3, or video on Amazon’s servers; when an owner clicked “resell,” the server transferred the access rights and download pointer to the buyer and simultaneously blocked the seller from any further access, so only one usable copy ever existed [1].  
2) Although the patent drew press coverage, music labels and publishers signaled opposition and no studio deals emerged, so Amazon has never launched the service; the idea remains unimplemented a decade later [2].

Apple  
1) Apple’s 2013 patent application (Pub. No. 20130159989) described an iTunes mechanism in which a “usage rights module” would revoke the seller’s key while issuing a new key to the buyer; the file itself could reside on a central server or on the seller’s device, but cryptographic controls ensured the original copy became inaccessible at the moment of transfer [3].  
2) The recording industry’s trade group (RIAA) and book publishers quickly argued the plan would undercut their ability to offer variable pricing, and Apple never deployed the feature; resale of digital purchases remains barred under current iTunes terms [4].

ReDigi  
1) Launched in 2011, ReDigi 2.0 required users to run software that performed a “verification” scan and then moved the eligible iTunes file to ReDigi’s cloud locker while simultaneously deleting every trace on the seller’s computer and connected devices; the buyer later downloaded that lone cloud copy, so only a single instance was present at any time [5].  
2) Capitol Records sued, claiming infringement.  In 2013 Judge Richard Sullivan ruled that ReDigi’s upload created an unauthorized reproduction, and the Second Circuit affirmed in 2018.  The decision effectively shut down the marketplace and signaled that first-sale does not extend to duplicative transfers of digital files under current law [6].

## References  
[1] U.S. Patent 8,364,595, “Secondary market for digital objects,” issued Feb. 5 2013.  
[2] Chris Welch, “Amazon awarded patent for ‘used’ digital content marketplace,” The Verge, Feb. 6 2013.  
[3] U.S. Patent App. 20130159989, “Managing access to digital content items,” published June 20 2013.  
[4] Ben Sisario, “Apple Seeks Patent for Reselling Digital Goods,” The New York Times, Feb. 7 2013.  
[5] Ryan Singel, “Start-Up ReDigi Wants to Create a Marketplace for Your Used MP3s,” Wired, Oct. 11 2011.  
[6] Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc., 934 F.3d 594 (2d Cir. 2019); SDNY opinion, 2013 WL 8659.
rounds.answer_b:
The two major technology retailers were **Amazon** and **Apple**, and the start-up was **ReDigi** [1][2][3].

**Amazon** sought a patent for a “secondary market for digital objects.” Its proposed system would allow a user to transfer a digital item—such as an e-book, song, video, or app—to another user by moving the entitlement and making the file inaccessible to the original owner, so that only one usable copy remained after the transfer [1][4]. The reaction was mostly anticipatory and controversial rather than judicial: the patent application drew widespread attention because it suggested a digital first-sale marketplace, but it also raised obvious copyright and licensing concerns, especially for e-books and media governed by restrictive platform terms [1][4].

**Apple** also pursued patent protection for a system designed to transfer ownership of digital media while ensuring exclusivity. Its filings described a method in which a digital item would be moved from one user’s account or device to another, with the seller’s access revoked or the original copy deleted so that just one authorized instance existed at a time [2][5]. Industry reaction was similarly cautious. Reports emphasized that the concept could create a used-digital marketplace, but the idea collided with publisher, label, and studio concerns about undermining primary sales and with the legal reality that many digital transactions are structured as licenses, not sales [2][5].

**ReDigi** actually launched such a marketplace for “used” iTunes songs. Its system attempted to verify ownership, migrate the music file packet-by-packet to ReDigi’s cloud, and delete residual copies from the seller’s computer so no duplicate remained in circulation [3][6]. Capitol Records sued. The federal court rejected ReDigi’s first-sale defense, holding that the process involved reproduction because a new copy was fixed on ReDigi’s servers; first sale protects redistribution of a particular lawfully made copy, not the making of a new one [3][6]. The ruling became a major setback for digital resale markets and reinforced the legal divide between physical and digital media [6].

## References
1. Wired, “Amazon’s Patent for a Digital Used Book Marketplace,” https://www.wired.com/2013/02/amazon-digital-used-goods-patent/  
2. The Guardian, “Apple patents system allowing users to resell digital media,” https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2013/mar/08/apple-patent-resell-digital-media  
3. Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc., 934 F. Supp. 2d 640 (S.D.N.Y. 2013), Justia: https://law.justia.com/cases/federal/district-courts/new-york/nysdce/1:2012cv00095/390576/109/  
4. U.S. Patent Application related to Amazon digital object transfers, Google Patents: https://patents.google.com/  
5. Apple patent reporting on digital resale/transfer systems, Tech industry coverage and patent summaries: https://patents.justia.com/assignee/apple-inc  
6. Harvard Journal of Law & Technology / case analyses discussing ReDigi and digital first sale: https://jolt.law.harvard.edu/2013/04/capitol-records-v-redigi-the-first-sale-doctrine-in-the-digital-age/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified Amazon, Apple, and ReDigi, and accurately described their connection to digital resale and first-sale-style arguments. Both pass the core entity check.

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent A provides more specific detail across all three entities. For Amazon and Apple, A includes patent numbers, more precise descriptions of how access rights or keys were revoked, and clearer descriptions of industry opposition (e.g., RIAA, publishers, lack of launch). For ReDigi, A goes further by noting both the district court ruling and the Second Circuit affirmation, giving a fuller legal arc. Agent B is accurate but slightly more generalized—industry reactions are described in broader terms, and the appellate affirmation in ReDigi is omitted.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Both are well-structured and readable. Agent B uses bold formatting effectively and is slightly more concise, but Agent A offers greater legal and technical specificity, which better satisfies the prompt’s request for how each system worked and the precise legal/industry response. Since neither contains factual errors, and A is more comprehensive, A provides the stronger overall search-style experience.

Therefore, Agent A wins on completeness and depth, with no major failures from Agent B.
